definition: A binary search tree is a binary tree where every node keeps smaller values in the left subtree and larger values in the right subtree.
deepExplanation: A BST turns comparison into direction. At each node, compare the target value with the current value. Smaller means move left; larger means move right. This can make search, insert, and delete efficient when the tree is balanced, but a badly ordered insertion sequence can make the tree behave like a linked list.

codeExamples.title: BST search

diagrams.caption: Each comparison removes about half the remaining tree when the tree is balanced.

commonMistakes: Assuming every BST operation is always O(log n) even when the tree is unbalanced.
Putting equal values inconsistently and breaking search rules.
Deleting a node with two children without preserving the in-order order.
practiceQuestions: Where would value 6 go in a BST whose root is 8 and left child is 3?
Why can a sorted insertion order make a BST slow?
